title: Moments of Margulis Functions and Values of Ternary Quadratic Forms
speaker: Wooyeon Kim
abstract:
The Oppenheim conjecture, proved by Margulis in 1986, states that for a non-degenerate indefinite irrational quadratic form Q in n≥
 3 variables, the image set Q(Zn) of integral vectors is a dense subset of the real line. Determining the distribution of values of an indefinite quadratic form at integral points asymptotically is referred to as quantitative Oppenheim conjecture. The quantitative Oppenheim conjecture was established by Eskin, Margulis, and Mozes for quadratic forms in n≥ 4 variables. In this talk, we discuss the quantitative Oppenheim conjecture for ternary quadratic forms (n=3). The main ingredient of the proof is a uniform boundedness result for the moments of Margulis functions over expanding translates of a unipotent orbit in the space of 3-dimensional lattices, under suitable Diophantine conditions of the initial unipotent orbit.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Oppenheim problem and homogeneous-dynamics dictionary
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up indefinite irrational quadratic forms, recalls Oppenheim’s density conjecture, and identifies the ternary case with the behavior of H=SO(2,1) orbits in SL_3(R)/SL_3(Z), where Margulis’s orbit-closure theorem gives the qualitative density result.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative counting problem and low-signature obstructions
proof_stages.summary: The qualitative question is refined to asymptotics for the number of integer vectors of norm at most T whose quadratic-form values lie in a fixed interval, with the expected volume main term modified in signatures (2,1) and (2,2) by rational-approximation, isotropic-subspace, and degenerate-plane obstructions.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of counting to unbounded equidistribution
proof_stages.summary: For T=e^t the counting function is expressed as an average of a Siegel transform along a long a_tu_r orbit in the space of lattices, so the lower bound follows from bounded equidistribution after cutoff while the upper bound requires controlling the cusp contribution of an unbounded test function.

proof_stages.label: Margulis height functions and the key moment theorem
proof_stages.summary: Margulis functions alpha_i and alpha are introduced to measure distance to the cusp and dominate relevant Siegel transforms, and the central needed estimate is a uniform lambda-moment bound for alpha(a_tu_r Delta_q) with some lambda>1 under Diophantine and non-isotropy hypotheses.

proof_stages.label: Classical Margulis inequality and its first-moment barrier
proof_stages.summary: The Eskin-Mozes-Margulis argument supplies a contracting averaged inequality for alpha^lambda only when lambda<1, whose iteration gives sub-first-moment nondivergence but fails precisely when lambda exceeds one.

proof_stages.label: Modified height and exceptional almost-isotropic lattices
proof_stages.summary: A modified height alpha-tilde comparable to alpha^{1+3delta} is constructed so that a Margulis-type contraction holds outside a small exceptional set of lattices containing vectors close to the light cone, with the modification encoded by a local weight involving distance to that cone.

proof_stages.label: Effective avoidance and iteration to quantitative Oppenheim
proof_stages.summary: The long unipotent trajectory is discretized as an iterated random walk, effective avoidance bounds the time spent in the exceptional set, and the modified inequality on the good pieces yields the required uniform moment bound, hence unbounded equidistribution and the quantitative Oppenheim asymptotic in signature (2,1).

examples.name: badly approximable binary quadratic form
examples.note: A binary form such as x^2-(1+sqrt(3))^2y^2 has zero isolated in its integer values, showing why the Oppenheim density statement requires at least three variables.
examples.engagement_reason: the binary quadratic form is given only as a counterexample, with the isolation of zero attributed to bad approximability rather than worked out

passes.reason: A badly approximable coefficient is a worst-approximation/critical Diophantine case, and the binary form is used to show the Oppenheim statement fails for n=2.
A binary form with a badly approximable coefficient is a Diophantine worst-approximation case used to show the n=2 density statement fails.
badly approximable binary forms are Diophantine-extreme cases and are used here to show the n=2 Oppenheim statement fails
A badly approximable binary form is a low-dimensional/Diophantine boundary case and is used to show Oppenheim-style density fails for n=2.
Used to show Oppenheim-type density fails in two variables, and bad approximability is an extremal Diophantine obstruction rather than a sporadic or degenerate form.
Used to show the n=2 Oppenheim statement fails, with the badly approximable coefficient giving an extremal Diophantine obstruction.
It is used to show the Oppenheim statement fails in two variables, and bad approximability is a worst-approximable Diophantine extreme.
It is used to show the Oppenheim statement fails in two variables, and the badly approximable coefficient is a Diophantine worst-case feature.
It is used to show Oppenheim-type density fails in two variables, and badly approximable coefficients are a Diophantine worst-approximation boundary case.
Used to show the Oppenheim statement fails in two variables, and the badly approximable coefficient is an extremal Diophantine obstruction keeping zero isolated.
Used to disprove the n=2 Oppenheim analogue, with a badly approximable coefficient giving a Diophantine worst-case form where zero is isolated.
It is used to show the Oppenheim statement fails for n=2, and the badly approximable coefficient is a worst-approximable Diophantine choice.

examples.name: Eisenstein series and theta functions as unbounded tests
examples.note: These familiar automorphic unbounded functions are mentioned only to illustrate that the same cusp-control problem occurs beyond Siegel transforms.
examples.engagement_reason: Eisenstein series and theta functions are cited as other unbounded test functions leading to the same question, with no analysis of them

passes.reason: Eisenstein series and theta functions are cited as ordinary unbounded automorphic test functions motivating the needed growth/non-divergence estimates, not as failures of a claim.

examples.name: algebraic-coefficient ternary form
examples.note: A form such as x_1x_3+y^2-sqrt(2)z^2, and more generally a non-rational form with algebraic coefficients, makes concrete the Diophantine condition of not being too well approximated by rational forms.
examples.engagement_reason: an algebraic-coefficient ternary form is offered as a simple example satisfying the Diophantine non-closeness assumption, but this is only asserted using a general fact about algebraic numbers

passes.reason: An irrational ternary quadratic form with algebraic coefficients is given as a standard example satisfying the not-too-close-to-rational hypothesis.
An irrational ternary form with algebraic coefficients is a systematic Diophantine example satisfying the hypotheses.
an irrational ternary form with algebraic coefficients is a nondegenerate systematic example satisfying the Diophantine hypotheses, not a failure case
